messages.content:
You are a Screenwriting Analyst. Richard Curtis is a prominent figure in the world of screenplay writing, well-known for his unique techniques and narrative styles. Could you help us understand and analyze Curtis's approach to the screenwriting process, commonly used narrative structures, themes, and insights into his writing process? Please write in-depth, insightful, valuable and informative as much as possible. Quoting direct quotes straight from the video transcripts with quotation marks as examples and/or visuals of points made. Here are the video transcripts "Video: Richard Curtis - "I Rewrote 'Four Weddings' 19 Times!" | BAFTA Guru – YouTube 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O3u9mR9qy_k&t=107s&ab_channel=BAFTAGuru 
Transcript:
“GETTING INTO THE FILM INDUSTRY
Question: How did you get started in the industry?
Richard Curtis: Actually in terms of getting into the industry at all my dad who was quite a serious man said that I had one year of fooling around and then I had to enter the marketing department at Unilever and in my first year I and I think 546 pounds or something like that but I then luckily got my first TV commission and that got me through the door as far as he was concerned and I got an agent actually by someone seeing me performing up in Edinburgh I got very lucky just that some lovely keen American woman who was living in London liked my jokes I got started in television by hanging onto the coattails of Rowan Atkinson who when he was 20 was a genius and it was obvious that he was going to be a TV star and I was his closest friend and wrote things for him and so we drifted in as comedians tend to by doing stage stuff of our own up in Edinburgh and then did a radio show which the traditional radio for which was pretty poor and then started writing sketches for a sketch show which became not the local news and then I wrote for that for for series so it was a very traditional route and then from a sketch show I went into a sitcom and then from a sitcom I went into films.
INFLUENCES
Question: What film or programme has influenced you the most?
Richard Curtis: John Cleese in many ways weirdly is the most influential person in my career because when I was young Monty Python was all there was 50% of your conversation with friends was just quoting Monty Python sketches so as it were like a generation before would have had the goons and the generation after might have had you know the young months as it were for me it was Monty Python and that's what made me interested in humor and started me writing sketches and then strangely the reason we wrote Blackadder was because Fawlty Towers have been so extraordinary that when I tried to write a modern sitcom it just looked so pathetic in comparison to 40 does that we only wrote historical one to avoid Fawlty Towers but the big influence on my films would probably have to be Woody Allen just because I so loved his films when I was young and he made films changed style and developed so quickly so you were always interested by what he was going to do next and he gave me the confidence to try different things and it was particularly for me had written silly sketches and a silly sitcom so Woody Allen did silly films when he started out at Scotland from sketches and ended up writing Annie Hall which was much closer to what I loved and the first film of his that I really loved so me then having the confidence to arrive for weddings may have been based on my passion for our confidence gained from Woody Allen.
ADVICE
Question: What advice would you give to someone starting out?
Richard Curtis: My obvious piece of advice is to make sure you write or do what you really like there was a bit of a pressure on me when I started they would say you know British rails funny or you know dreadful sandwiches that you buy and M&S a funny write joke about that and I think it's really important to do what you think is interesting not what other people tell you might be interesting for me writing to sort of get work because you know that sort of work is popular as a disaster so I would say right from the heart and hope that somebody likes it as a sort of person in films I suppose my obvious thing would be to take every stage of the process incredibly seriously and I would say to get very involved so I had a script editor who's also my girlfriend who made me rewrite “Four Weddings” 19 times so I didn't just handed something that I thought might be okay it would be ruthlessly dealt with I then worked with a director who was obsessed by casting and the casting process took forever so actually once the movie had been cast it was almost made and then I think the final thing is that when you're editing you can fix most films I mean at least three of my films have been when finished the first really careful cut of being spectacular disasters really really bad failed movies and we managed just by determination and thinking you know things through in a different way to make them eventually work by hook or by crook so those are some of my pieces of advice.”



Video: Script to Screen Red Carpet: LOVE ACTUALLY with Richard Curtis | Matt Ryan – YouTube
https://youtu.be/NbYecUZHgy4 
Transcript:
“Interviewer: I have question for you when you when you take something like “Love Actually” which i love dearly uh when you have like a multiple protagonist how do you balance like you know the screen time could you you have to cut some things you probably really cared about you know because you have multiple characters to explore.
Richard Curtis: Well “Love Actually” of all the films i've written is the one that turned out most wrong after we'd shot it so in fact i think it's very hard to judge what you've got to try and do is write good stuff for all of them and then actually remake the order and remake the dynamic of it once it's shot that's what we did on “Love Actually” the the script i wrote had all the same contents but in a completely different order to the finished film.
Interviewer: So something like “Notting Hill” was a little easier for you basically two characters
Richard Curtis: She walks into the room she kisses him she goes away but in this one it was like playing three-dimensional chess because after every scene you could put any scene from any of the other 10 stories.
Interviewer: So and maybe on uh when you do an ensemble piece and it's more the director on set which we directed of course has a lot more to do trying to rework the script at the same time
Richard Curtis: no no i think they've got a lot to do in the edit that would be what i have to say i think they've got a lot to do in the edit in some ways they're great fun to do because you only have to work for a week or two with each actor so they never see through you and realize what a bad director you are.”


Video: Richard Curtis On Creating A Classic Rom-Com | Fast Company - YouTube
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FENpwVe14MA 
Transcript:
“INTRO:
(00:00) - Seeing as how love is quite a big subject. And I often think about serial killers. There've only been about eight of them. And yet, there are about 100 movies about them. And yet, every day a million people are in love, so I can't believe that romantic films won't, sort of, stay at the core of cinema, in one way or another.
(00:17) (cheerful music)
NOTTING HILL
- I do solemnly declare. - I do solemnly declare. - [Priest] That I know not of any lawful impediment. - That I know not of any lawful impediment. - Why I, Lydia. - [Groom] Why I, Bernard. - "Four Weddings," which is thought of as a romantic comedy, I didn't see as that. I thought of it as a semi-autobiographical film about love and friendship.
(00:51) I wasn't really aware that there was genre or a rule book. Then I became more aware, with "Notting Hill," that that was what I was up to. Every Thursday, I used to go and have dinner with my friends, Piers and Paula. And I just thought, if I turned up at Piers and Paula's, if Madonna was my date, how would they react? Helen Fielding, who then wrote Bridget Jones, obsessive Madonna fan.
(01:16) She would scream a lot and lose her cool. And neither Piers, nor Paula, would have any idea who she was. And that exact scene actually happens in "Notting Hill". - Last film you did, what'd you get paid? - 15 million dollars. - Right. - The curious thing about "Notting Hll" is that, even though Hugh had been the star, Hugh Grant had been the star of "Four Weddings," I didn't want Hugh Grant.
(01:40) I very specifically didn't want him. Because I thought it would so fantastic to have a complete unknown, which would really have made the movie be like a perfect mirror to the story itself. But we auditioned about 10 people, and I thought, oh god, I'll never find anyone who's any good. We might as well cast Hugh.
(01:59) I'm quite embarrassed by "I'm just a girl, "standing in front of a boy," because I clearly thought it was a good line. Because in the next scene, one of the characters says to Hugh Grant, "what did she say?" And he says, "oh, she's just--" - A girl, standing in front of a boy, asking him to love her.
(02:17) - So I obviously thought, I better repeat it, like it was going back to the chorus, or the hook, in a song.
BRIDGET JONES
(upbeat music) Well, the terrifying thing about being involved in "Bridget Jones" was actually Helen Fielding, because she was my best friend; it was her book. And I'd actually read "Bridget" seven times before it came out because she gave it to me with every extra 50 pages.
(02:40) We cast Renee, and we made the film. And then we had trouble editing the film. And Helen was full of, "that's not quite like Bridget. "This isn't quite right." And then you realize the big revelation to making movies, that there comes a point where you've gotta stop thinking about your source, as it were, stop thinking about the script you've written, or the book that the script you've written is based on, and say our Bridget is Renee.
(03:04) And the moment that we accepted that, and all the wonderful qualities that she brought to the film, some of which might not be the exact representation of the book, but was a fully-fledged, fabulous human. You then edit the movie towards what you've got, and that means that, in some ways, it's better for that, because you've got an embodiment of a special, cinematic Bridget, rather than an attempt to just reproduce what was in the book.
LOVE ACTUALLY
(03:33) ♪ I'll take you down ♪ ♪ I'll take you down ♪ ♪ Where no one's ever gone ♪ - Yeah. - I mean, "Love Actually" was partly inspired by the fact that I had done these three romantic comedies before, and I, in fact, didn't want to write another one. The plot in that movie, of Hugh and Martine, was a whole film I thought of doing.
(03:54) And the plot of Colin Firth's story was a whole film that I had intended to write. So, what I was trying to do was, it was push the romantic comedy genre as far as it could possibly go. And just take the three best scenes of 10 different films and shove them all together. The big inspiration was "Nashville," a Robert Altman movie, and "Short Cuts," a Robert Altman Movie.
(04:17) Which are very standards to be aiming for, because these are two of my favorite movies of all time. These movies with a lot of plots are tough to do. "Love Actually," in its first edit, was the third worst film of all time. I mean it was really the worst example I can remember of something which had worked brilliantly in a read-though, not working well.
(04:39) So it was like playing three-dimensional chess, because any scene could go after any other scene, from any other point, in any of the other stories. So, that was very, very hard work. I was a new director. And if it had been a movie in which there was a cast who would've had 10 weeks to get used to me, maybe they would've got used to me.
(04:58) But we only had one week per plot. So I felt I was auditioning for the actors every single week. What I realized was quite how unfair I'd been on the directors that I'd worked with beforehand. Because there's this terrible contradiction in movies, that a writer's got two years to fix absolutely everything, and then the director's got one day in which to shoot it.
(05:21) And suddenly I understood why sometimes the directors had rushed through things I thought was important or been happy when they got it. Because I was always saying, oh, let's have another go, let's try it another way. - I'd only give one piece of advice to anyone marrying: try and marry someone kind.
(05:39) - The original inspiration for "About Time" is the scene that most people talk to me about, which is the scene on a beach, when the leading character travels back to just spend an afternoon with his dad who's just died. And, almost for the first time in the movies, the idea I had was based on a visual thing.
(05:58) And I remember starting now, at last, as a writer, to start to sort of think, well, what really matters about that scene isn't so much all the words that they're saying, but where they're standing, that it's a lonely street. So I think, at last, by "About Time" I was gaining a bit of visual confidence.
(06:17) And I hired the cameraman from my favorite visual movie, called "Like Crazy," that Drake Doremus made. And I started to think, if you want to fall in love with a character, then the words can do two-thirds of it, but if you can shoot it well, then maybe that's gonna be the thing which, as it were, goes into your heart without knowing how it got there.
(06:42) ♪ Oh, I believe in yesterday ♪ - Oh my, when did you write that? - I didn't write it; Paul McCartney wrote it, the Beatles. - Who? - I mean I've been obsessed by the Beatles for the whole of my life. That's the real inspiration. But then, of course, the inspiration that the Beatles gave me was that a lot of their stuff was about love.
(07:08) We got permission, fabulously, to use, I think, 18 Beatles' songs, and they didn't say which. It was a wonderful thing to write, because I'd be typing along and think, okay, he's having a crisis. And then I'd go through the Beatles catalog and think, well, "Help" is clearly, if he's gonna sing any song now, it should be "Help.
(07:26) " It was a wonderful thing 'cause they've got such a range that, as I told this tale, there was always a perfect Beatles' song to go in. I think that the curve of my career is to realize that love doesn't end at the first kiss or as you walk down the aisle in wedding. That's where some of my movies did end.
(07:46) When I went to "Love Actually," which had brothers and sisters, and fathers and sons, and married couples. And then, "About Time" was a romantic comedy for half of it and then a film about death and family and marriage. And that "Yesterday" is a movie about love of music and love.
(08:03) I think I've learned that love's a big subject that the traditional shape of a rom-com doesn't quite cover.”

Be as much detailed as possible. PLEASE

Analyzing Richard Curtis's approach to screenwriting offers a fascinating insight into the mind of one of Britain's most beloved storytellers. Known for iconic films such as "Four Weddings and a Funeral," "Notting Hill," and "Love Actually," Curtis has carved a unique niche in the realm of romantic comedies, blending humor with deep emotional resonance. Through the various transcripts provided, key aspects of his narrative style, themes, and writing process emerge, showcasing a writer deeply committed to exploring love, friendship, and the human condition, all while maintaining a distinctly British flair.

### Narrative Structures and Techniques

Curtis's films often utilize innovative narrative structures to engage the audience. For example, "Love Actually" employs a multi-narrative format, interweaving various love stories into a single cohesive narrative. This approach, inspired by Robert Altman's "Nashville" and "Short Cuts," as Curtis mentions, allows for a rich exploration of love's various facets. It's not merely about romantic love but encompasses familial, platonic, and unrequited love. Curtis's adeptness at this complex structure speaks to his assertion that writing "Love Actually" was like "playing three-dimensional chess," highlighting his skill in balancing multiple storylines without losing coherence or emotional impact.

### Themes

Curtis's work is permeated with recurring themes, most notably love, friendship, and the intrinsic beauty of everyday life. In discussing "About Time," Curtis reflects on moving beyond traditional romantic comedy endings, exploring love's broader scope, including family and personal growth. This theme is a testament to his belief in love's omnipresence and significance in diverse forms, a sentiment echoed in his musings on the universality of love and its rightful place at the core of cinema.

Another recurring theme is the ordinary juxtaposed with the extraordinary. In "Notting Hill," the mundane life of a bookstore owner is upended by his love affair with a famous actress, a premise born from Curtis's simple yet ingenious thought experiment about bringing Madonna home to dinner. This interplay between the everyday and the extraordinary allows Curtis to delve into themes of identity, fame, and the transformative power of love.

### Writing Process

Curtis's writing process reveals a blend of meticulous planning and adaptive creativity. His admission of rewriting "Four Weddings" 19 times underscores a dedication to refinement and perfection. Meanwhile, the genesis of "Notting Hill" during the filming of "Four Weddings" shows how ideas can simmer and evolve over time. This mix of perseverance and spontaneity, coupled with a deep involvement in all stages of film production—writing, casting, directing, and editing—showcases Curtis's holistic view of storytelling.

Curtis also emphasizes the importance of writing from the heart, a sentiment that reflects in his advice to aspiring writers. He champions authenticity and personal connection to one's work, suggesting that the most compelling stories come from a place of genuine interest and affection. This ethos is evident in his heartfelt exploration of themes close to his experience and worldview, making his films resonate on a profoundly personal level with audiences.

### Social Commentary and Evolving Perspectives

Curtis's films are not without their critics, as highlighted in discussions about the lack of diversity in his earlier work and the portrayal of certain characters. His reflective stance on these critiques, considering his "limited experience of life" outside certain socio-economic bubbles, indicates an awareness and willingness to engage with critical feedback. This openness to reassessment underscores the evolving nature of storytelling and the importance of diverse perspectives in enriching narrative cinema.

### Conclusion

Richard Curtis's contribution to screenwriting is marked by his unique blend of humor, emotional depth, and innovative storytelling. His narrative techniques, thematic explorations, and iterative writing process illustrate a creator deeply in tune with the human experience. Curtis's films remind us of love's myriad forms and the beauty inherent in the everyday, making his work a significant touchstone in the landscape of British cinema.

Analyzing Richard Curtis's approach to screenwriting and directing provides a broad view of his impact on British cinema and international audiences. His work, characterized by a unique blend of wit, romance, and poignant moments, has made significant contributions to the romantic comedy genre. From iconic films like "Four Weddings and a Funeral" to "Love Actually," Curtis's distinctive narrative voice resonates through his exploration of love, the absurdity of life, and the beauty of everyday moments.

### Approach to Screenwriting

Curtis's screenwriting process, as he reveals, is organic and somewhat elusive, even to himself. He stresses the importance of belief in the story, highlighting that a narrative should continually draw back to its core purpose (CNBC International TV Interview). This approach ensures that his films carry a strong thematic spine, often centered around love in its myriad forms. Curtis's transition from being the "most ill-disciplined writer" to adopting a more structured routine due to family commitments speaks to his adaptability and the evolving nature of creative processes.

### Character Development

Curtis has a fondness for creating "stupid" characters that provide comedic relief while also endearing themselves to the audience. Characters like Baldrick in "Blackadder," Emma Chambers in "The Vicar of Dibley," and Rhys Ifans in "Notting Hill" showcase his ability to use humor to deepen the narrative and enhance the relatability of his stories (CNBC International TV Interview). These characters often find themselves in ludicrous situations, which not only serve as comic relief but also underscore Curtis's belief in the value of every narrative's levity.

### Collaborative Efforts

A significant aspect of Curtis's work ethic is his emphasis on collaboration with individuals he genuinely admires, highlighting the potential pitfalls of working with 'shiny objects' - i.e., famous names that may not necessarily align with the project’s vision (CNBC International TV Interview). This insight into his process not only acknowledges the importance of synergy in creative ventures but also serves as advice to emerging screenwriters, emphasizing integrity over fame.

### Evolution of Ideas

Curtis's narrative ideas evolve from simple concepts to complex, layered stories, often incorporating a blend of genres to convey the richness of human experiences. For instance, his simple wish to write about appreciating a normal day evolved into the time-travel movie "About Time," blending science fiction with a profound exploration of happiness and the beauty of ordinary life (AMC Interview). This reflects Curtis's ability to infuse depth and meaning into seemingly straightforward narratives.

### Editing and the Creative Process

Editing emerges as Curtis's favorite part of the filmmaking process, underscoring its role in transforming the written script into the final cinematic experience. He describes how films can shift dramatically from the initial cut to the final product through editing, emphasizing the necessity of adaptation and the removal of segments that don't serve the overarching story effectively. Notably, "Love Actually" underwent significant changes during this process, with Curtis comparing the challenge to playing "three-dimensional chess" due to its multiple interlinking storylines (Collider Interviews).

### Contributions to Cinema

Richard Curtis's contributions to cinema extend beyond his narrative ingenuity to include insights into the collaborative nature of filmmaking, the evolutionary trajectory of stories from concept to screen, and the malleability required during the editing process. His enduring themes of love, human connection, and the idiosyncrasies of life remain central to his appeal, ensuring his films' place in both British and international cinematic landscapes. Through his work, Curtis invites audiences to find joy and resonance in the sprawling messiness of life, articulated through his unique lens of humor, empathy, and emotional depth.

**Title: The Narrative Craft of Richard Curtis: An In-depth Analysis**

**[Opening Frame]**

[Cut to a montage of Richard Curtis's films—rain splattering on a window from "Four Weddings and a Funeral," a bustling London street in "Notting Hill," and the iconic cue card scene from "Love Actually." Soft, sentimental music plays in the background.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"In the world of cinema, few screenwriters have carved a niche as distinct and warmly regarded as Richard Curtis. Today, we delve deep into the narrative craft and storytelling ethos of a man who, quite literally, defined a genre."

**[Fade to black, then cut to the title screen]**

"The Narrative Craft of Richard Curtis: An In-depth Analysis"

**[Segment 1: Introduction]**

[Cut to clips from various Curtis films while the narrator speaks.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"From the comedic mishaps of a British bachelor in 'Four Weddings and a Funeral' to the interwoven love stories of 'Love Actually,' Richard Curtis's films have become synonymous with a uniquely British blend of humor, warmth, and unabashed romance. But what lies beneath the surface of Curtis's seemingly effortless charm and wit?"

**[Segment 2: Curtis's Screenwriting Process]**

[Cut to Richard Curtis in interviews, discussing his process.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"Curtis's approach to screenwriting is as refreshing as it is unconventional. Describing his process as 'organic,' Curtis reveals a deep belief in the stories he tells, living with them, much like one would in a relationship, long before they ever manifest on paper."

**[On-screen quote: Richard Curtis, BAFTA Guru Interview]**

"Films can't be infatuations. They've got to be relationships."

**[Voiceover]:**

"His is a canvas painted with the colors of life's everyday moments, finding humor and profound meaning in the ordinary."

**[Segment 3: Themes of Love and Humanity]**

[Cut to clips of pivotal romantic scenes from Curtis's films.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"At the heart of every Curtis film lies the exploration of love—not just romantic love but the love of family and friends. Films like 'Notting Hill' and 'About Time' showcase Curtis's fascination with the complexities and simplicities of the human heart."

**[On-screen quote: Richard Curtis, Rotten Tomatoes Interview]**

"The things that got me through: Love, family, and friendship."

**[Voiceover]:**

"His work invites audiences to cherish the beauty of a normal day, propelling the seemingly mundane into the extraordinary through the lens of love."

**[Segment 4: Curtis on Writing and Editing]**

[Cut to Curtis speaking at BAFTA, superimposed with scenes being edited from his movies.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"Beyond writing, Curtis emphasizes the paramount importance of being intricately involved in the editing process, reshaping and refining the narrative, much like 'rewriting' the film."

**[On-screen quote: Richard Curtis, BAFTA Screenwriters' Lecture]**

"A huge amount of the challenge is about control... about not wasting your life making or having other people make the wrong films."

**[Voiceover]:**

"This hands-on approach has seen Curtis navigate his films through turbulent waters, transforming 'catastrophic' first cuts into the cherished classics we know today."

**[Segment 5: Legacy and Influence]**

[Cut to various awards ceremonies and Curtis receiving acclaim.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"Richard Curtis's influence on the romantic comedy genre is undeniable. His films offer more than just escapist fantasies; they are heartfelt explorations of the human condition, replete with all its foibles and finesse."

**[On-screen quote: Richard Curtis, BAFTA Guru Interview]**

"I just wrote a lot about love, friends, and family because, if you really write what you care about, you have no choice."

**[Voiceover]:**

"In a Curtis film, every laugh, every tear, and every moment of joy is a testament to his enduring belief in the power of storytelling to uplift, to heal, and to celebrate the extraordinary within the ordinary."

**[Closing Frame]**

[Montage of the final scenes of Curtis's films, their characters laughing, hugging, and finding peace.]

**[Voiceover]:**

"Richard Curtis has not just written movies; he has crafted enduring narratives that resonate with the universal experiences of love, loss, and laughter. In doing so, he reminds us to cherish the 'normal day,' to seek out the 'warm fuzzies' in our lives, and to always, always follow our hearts."

[The music swells one final time before fading out.]

**[Text on Screen]:**

"Thank you for watching. We hope this journey through the narrative world of Richard Curtis has inspired you to celebrate the simple, the sweet, and the profoundly human moments in life."

**[Fade to black.]**

2. Identify and define the writer’s approach to the screenwriting process?


Curtis has described himself as the ‘luckiest man in movies’ for having the chance to write multiple redrafts – with the assistance of his partner Emma Freud – and the power to influence casting choices, select the director, and to intervene during the shooting and editing process.
He has also made no denial that his scripts are largely based on his own career and personal experiences.
He only writes ‘on something he was a hundred percent sure of and no one in the world knew more about than him’.

3. What narrative structures and themes does the writer typically use within their writing and films?


Curtis-ian elements of cinematic comedy were present right from the start. First, there is the classic “fish out of water” hero…Next, there is the “love interest,” a thoroughly modern young woman…And finally, there is the “last act dash”...Those same essential elements, held together by an apparently unceasing stream of jokes and one-liners... would be used by Curtis in his hugely successful romantic comedies.
That there are enough narrative, thematic and tonal similarities between all of his major films to establish Curtis as an auteur is usually taken as a given...a philosophy about love and human dependency that had simmered throughout the entire Curtis oeuvre.
Romantic comedy is the main genre, but Curtis injects social commentary and explores cultural clashes.
He uses elements like pop culture references, music, and high/low culture clashes to generate humor and drama. (e.g., contrasting references to David Cassidy and John Lennon with the National Film Theatre in "Four Weddings")
There's some variation in tone and style between films ("The Girl in the Cafe" vs. "The Boat That Rocked").
There's a recurring theme of the value and commoditization of art (e.g., "Bean" with Whistler's Mother painting).

6. Identify and Analyse at least one of the writers well-known screenplays and discuss the different strategies and approaches to their writing, including:


Movie: FOUR WEDDINGS AND A FUNERAL (1994)


Logline: Over the course of five social occasions, a committed bachelor must consider the notion that he may have discovered love.


Synopsis: Lovable Englishman Charles (Hugh Grant) and his group of friends seem to be unlucky in love. When Charles meets a beautiful American named Carrie (Andie MacDowell) at a wedding, he thinks his luck may have changed. But, after one magical night, Carrie returns to the States, ending what might have been. As Charles and Carrie's paths continue to cross - over a handful of nuptials and one funeral - he comes to believe they are meant to be together, even if their timing always seems to be off.


1. Structure
Organization: The film is structured around five major events - four weddings and a funeral.
Settings: Primarily takes place in England, with weddings at various locations and a recurring pub where the friends gather.
Characters:
An ensemble cast of friends in their 30s, including the commitment-phobic Charles (Hugh Grant)
The American beauty Carrie (Andie MacDowell)
A supportive group with their own romantic entanglements.
There's a notable inclusion of a healthy gay couple, Gareth (Simon Callow) and Matthew (John Hannah).
Stage Directions: not applicable
Special Features: The film's charm lies in its witty dialogue and relatable characters, not special effects.

2. Story

Given Circumstances: Charles, a charming but commitment-averse man, finds himself repeatedly drawn to Carrie at weddings.
Dramatic Action: The story follows Charles' emotional journey as he navigates missed opportunities, self-doubt, and ultimately, pursuing true love. A central event is the unexpected death of a friend, Gareth, which adds a layer of grief and reflection.

3. Pace & Character Arcs

Pace: The film cleverly balances humor and emotional moments, with a light and breezy pace at the weddings, punctuated by the more serious funeral scene.
Character Status & Function:
Charles: Protagonist, overcoming his fear of commitment.
Carrie: Love interest, initially hesitant about commitment.
Supporting characters: Each friend has their own quirks and romantic struggles, adding depth and humor.
Relationships: The film explores the complexities of friendship, love, and facing adulthood.
Objectives & Obstacles: Charles desires a committed relationship with Carrie, but his own indecisiveness and her past relationships create hurdles.
Character Arcs: Most characters experience growth. Charles learns to commit, Carrie embraces vulnerability, and the friends find solace in each other.

4. Text Structure & Dialogue

Text Structure: Dialogue-driven, with witty banter and emotional exchanges between characters.
Function of Dialogue: Reveals characters' personalities, drives the plot, and provides humor and emotional depth.
Word Choice: Clever, witty, and sprinkled with British slang.
Characteristics of Dialogue: Fast-paced, natural-sounding conversations that feel realistic.

5. Themes & Genre

Themes: Love, commitment, friendship, facing mortality, the importance of human connection.
Genre: Romantic comedy with dramatic elements.
Idea: Finding love and happiness can be messy and unpredictable, but true connection is worth the journey.
Philosophy: Life is a mix of joy, sorrow, and self-discovery.
Atmosphere: Upbeat and humorous, with moments of poignant reflection.
Central Image: The repeated motif of weddings and a funeral symbolizes life's milestones and the importance of cherishing relationships.
Audience: Primarily targets adults who can relate to the complexities of love and life in your 30s.

Additional information:
Screenwriter Richard Curtis's own experiences as a wedding attendee inspired Four Weddings and a Funeral. According to Curtis he began writing the script at age 34, after realising he had attended 65 weddings in an 11-year period. At one wedding he was propositioned by a fellow guest, but he turned her down and forever regretted it; accordingly he based the origin of Charles and Carrie's romance on that situation.

It took Curtis 19 drafts to reach the final version. He has commented on director Mike Newell's influence; "I come from a school where making it funny is what matters. Mike was obsessed with keeping it real. Every character, no matter how small, has a story, not just three funny lines. It's a romantic film about love and friendship that swims in a sea of jokes."

Curtis chose to omit any mention of the characters' careers, because he didn't think a group of friends would realistically discuss their jobs while together at a wedding.

Thompson A (1994) ''Four Weddings and a Funeral'' a surprise hit, Entertainment Weekly website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://ew.com/article/1994/05/06/four-weddings-and-funeral-surprise-hit/ 

Richard Whalley Anthony Curtis, CBE (born 8 November 1956) is New Zealand-born British screenwriter, music producer, actor and film director, known primarily for romantic comedy films such as Four Weddings and a Funeral, Bridget Jones's Diary, Notting Hill, and Love Actually, as well as the hit sitcoms Blackadder, Mr. Bean and The Vicar of Dibley. He is also the founder of the British charity Comic Relief.

Richard Curtis is probably the most successful comedy writer in the world due to one of his many characters: Mr. Bean. He created the 15-show series with actor Rowan Atkinson and it has become one of the most successful British cultural exports in history with hundreds of millions of fans worldwide. 
He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
His fascination with love stories began at age seven after he became infatuated with a girl who rejected him. He remained obsessed for two years. “I was always an absolute fool for love... somewhere deep inside, that is a problem," he said, adding. "Definitely, there was some psychological freakery there.' 
At 13, he won a scholarship to private school Harrow, where he excelled, and left as ‘head boy’. He also won a place at Oxford University where he became friends with Mr. Bean actor Rowan Atkinson and they worked together on Atkinson’s Edinburgh Fringe show.  
After graduation, Richard co-wrote the British comedy classic Blackadder, which ran through the '80s with Atkinson as its star. The program also cemented the comedy careers of Stephen Fry and Hugh Laurie. 
In 1989, Richard wrote his first comedy movie: The Tall Guy starring Jeff Goldblum and Emma Thompson. By the ‘90s he was a big name in British comedy and an international celebrity when his low-budget movie Four Weddings and a Funeral became a runaway success. He followed that with the first Mr. Bean movie, then broke box office records again with the Julia Roberts and Grant movie Notting Hill (1999).
 The hits kept on rolling though the 2000s as he has worked with stars such as Alan Rickman, Emma Thompson, Liam Neeson, and Dustin Hoffman.  
 He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
Richard’s charity work started almost by accident. Like many others, he’d been deeply affected by Live Aid. “I happened to go to dinner and met a girl who ran a charity and was about to go out to Ethiopia,” he said. He went with her and found the experience ‘startling’.  
“I was there towards the end of the famine, but I saw things of such horror that I couldn’t stand by and do nothing. You’d have a situation where people were separated into different corrugated iron huts - one would have people who were going to die that night, the next would be people who were probably going to die - and the last hut was people who might just survive.” 
He never got over it. When he returned to London, he decided to do everything he could to make comedy contribute to aid - from stage shows, to TV, to merchandise,  as if he were the CEO of a business. 

Comic Relief’s aim is to create a just world, free from poverty. That’s also the aim of the campaign group Make Poverty History, which Richard also founded. It’s a coalition of aid agencies and campaign groups that lobby to cancel Third World debt among other goals. He’s pointedly aware of the “slightly peculiar contradiction in my life between the Comic Relief side and the comedy writing side,” but has an explanation. 
“What we do on Red Nose Day is make things as funny as we can so that people can see the other side to all the sadness we show,” he said. “I suppose what we are fighting for is that everyone should have the privilege of leading a happy life and being able to laugh like the rest of us instead of being worried that their husband is going to beat them up or they are going to die of a mosquito bite.”
Richard considers Comic Relief and Make Poverty History to be his main priorities even at the expense of his personal life. “I help edit almost every appeal film we do,” he said in 2019.  “When I see a film about a child who hasn’t got a mosquito net, and the impact that makes, I’ll then write to Kiera Knightley and ask her to do something to help us scale the impact.”

--- 1. A very brief background of the writer and the films known for writing


Richard Curtis - who was born in New Zealand and moved to London when he was 11 - has been one of the most influential, profitable, and talked about figures in British comedy. 
The talented 67-year-old has written more rom-coms than Sainsbury's stocks popcorn for with classics such as Four Weddings and a Funeral, Notting Hill, and Love Actually all coming from his fair hand.
In addition to the epic filmography, Richard also penned Blackadder, Mr. Bean and The Vicar of Dibley.
When he's not tickling your funny bones, he is the co-founder of British charity Comic Relief along with comedian Lenny Henry.
As if that's not enough for his CV, Richard Curtis has also been awarded a CBE by the Queen, won two BAFTAs and been nominated for an Academy Award... Is there no stopping him?!


Windle L (2017) COMIC RELIEF LAUNCH Who’s on The One Show tonight? Richard Curtis joins Michelle Ackerley and Matt Baker to launch Red Nose Day 2017, The Sun website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://www.thesun.co.uk/tvandshowbiz/2749624/who-the-one-show-tonight-richard-curtis-michelle-ackerley-matt-baker-red-nose-day-2017/ 
Kavanagh J (2019) RICHARD, ACTUALLY How many films has Richard Curtis written and who is the Comic Relief founder’s partner Emma Freud?, The Sun website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://www.thesun.co.uk/tvandshowbiz/8515172/richard-curtis-films-comic-relief-founder-wife-emma-freud/ 


2. Identify and define the writer’s approach to the screenwriting process?


Curtis has described himself as the ‘luckiest man in movies’ for having the chance to write multiple redrafts – with the assistance of his partner Emma Freud – and the power to influence casting choices, select the director, and to intervene during the shooting and editing process.
He has also made no denial that his scripts are largely based on his own career and personal experiences.
He only writes ‘on something he was a hundred percent sure of and no one in the world knew more about than him’.


Leggott J (2012) ‘Travels in Curtisland: Richard Curtis and British Comedy Cinema’, British Comedy Cinema, 184-195, Routledge.


3. What narrative structures and themes does the writer typically use within their writing and films?


Curtis-ian elements of cinematic comedy were present right from the start. First, there is the classic “fish out of water” hero…Next, there is the “love interest,” a thoroughly modern young woman…And finally, there is the “last act dash”...Those same essential elements, held together by an apparently unceasing stream of jokes and one-liners... would be used by Curtis in his hugely successful romantic comedies.
That there are enough narrative, thematic and tonal similarities between all of his major films to establish Curtis as an auteur is usually taken as a given...a philosophy about love and human dependency that had simmered throughout the entire Curtis oeuvre.
Romantic comedy is the main genre, but Curtis injects social commentary and explores cultural clashes.
He uses elements like pop culture references, music, and high/low culture clashes to generate humor and drama. (e.g., contrasting references to David Cassidy and John Lennon with the National Film Theatre in "Four Weddings")
There's some variation in tone and style between films ("The Girl in the Cafe" vs. "The Boat That Rocked").
There's a recurring theme of the value and commoditization of art (e.g., "Bean" with Whistler's Mother painting).


Keady M (2020) The Great Screenwriters: Part 29 – Richard Curtis, The Script Lab website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://thescriptlab.com/features/screenwriting-101/11472-the-great-screenwriters-part-29-richard-curtis/ 
Leggott J (2012) ‘Travels in Curtisland: Richard Curtis and British Comedy Cinema’, British Comedy Cinema, 184-195, Routledge.


4. What insights do they have to the screenwriting process?


Curtis's insights into the screenwriting process highlight the importance of authenticity, personal connection to the material, and the power of collaboration. He values storytelling that resonates with audiences on an emotional level while incorporating elements of humor and wit.


Leggott J (2012) ‘Travels in Curtisland: Richard Curtis and British Comedy Cinema’, British Comedy Cinema, 184-195, Routledge.

6. Identify and Analyse at least one of the writers well-known screenplays and discuss the different strategies and approaches to their writing, including:


Movie: FOUR WEDDINGS AND A FUNERAL (1994)


Logline: Over the course of five social occasions, a committed bachelor must consider the notion that he may have discovered love.


Synopsis: Lovable Englishman Charles (Hugh Grant) and his group of friends seem to be unlucky in love. When Charles meets a beautiful American named Carrie (Andie MacDowell) at a wedding, he thinks his luck may have changed. But, after one magical night, Carrie returns to the States, ending what might have been. As Charles and Carrie's paths continue to cross - over a handful of nuptials and one funeral - he comes to believe they are meant to be together, even if their timing always seems to be off.


1. Structure
Organization: The film is structured around five major events - four weddings and a funeral.
Settings: Primarily takes place in England, with weddings at various locations and a recurring pub where the friends gather.
Characters:
An ensemble cast of friends in their 30s, including the commitment-phobic Charles (Hugh Grant)
The American beauty Carrie (Andie MacDowell)
A supportive group with their own romantic entanglements.
There's a notable inclusion of a healthy gay couple, Gareth (Simon Callow) and Matthew (John Hannah).
Stage Directions: not applicable
Special Features: The film's charm lies in its witty dialogue and relatable characters, not special effects.

2. Story

Given Circumstances: Charles, a charming but commitment-averse man, finds himself repeatedly drawn to Carrie at weddings.
Dramatic Action: The story follows Charles' emotional journey as he navigates missed opportunities, self-doubt, and ultimately, pursuing true love. A central event is the unexpected death of a friend, Gareth, which adds a layer of grief and reflection.

3. Pace & Character Arcs

Pace: The film cleverly balances humor and emotional moments, with a light and breezy pace at the weddings, punctuated by the more serious funeral scene.
Character Status & Function:
Charles: Protagonist, overcoming his fear of commitment.
Carrie: Love interest, initially hesitant about commitment.
Supporting characters: Each friend has their own quirks and romantic struggles, adding depth and humor.
Relationships: The film explores the complexities of friendship, love, and facing adulthood.
Objectives & Obstacles: Charles desires a committed relationship with Carrie, but his own indecisiveness and her past relationships create hurdles.
Character Arcs: Most characters experience growth. Charles learns to commit, Carrie embraces vulnerability, and the friends find solace in each other.

4. Text Structure & Dialogue

Text Structure: Dialogue-driven, with witty banter and emotional exchanges between characters.
Function of Dialogue: Reveals characters' personalities, drives the plot, and provides humor and emotional depth.
Word Choice: Clever, witty, and sprinkled with British slang.
Characteristics of Dialogue: Fast-paced, natural-sounding conversations that feel realistic.

5. Themes & Genre

Themes: Love, commitment, friendship, facing mortality, the importance of human connection.
Genre: Romantic comedy with dramatic elements.
Idea: Finding love and happiness can be messy and unpredictable, but true connection is worth the journey.
Philosophy: Life is a mix of joy, sorrow, and self-discovery.
Atmosphere: Upbeat and humorous, with moments of poignant reflection.
Central Image: The repeated motif of weddings and a funeral symbolizes life's milestones and the importance of cherishing relationships.
Audience: Primarily targets adults who can relate to the complexities of love and life in your 30s.

Additional information:
Screenwriter Richard Curtis's own experiences as a wedding attendee inspired Four Weddings and a Funeral. According to Curtis he began writing the script at age 34, after realising he had attended 65 weddings in an 11-year period. At one wedding he was propositioned by a fellow guest, but he turned her down and forever regretted it; accordingly he based the origin of Charles and Carrie's romance on that situation.

It took Curtis 19 drafts to reach the final version. He has commented on director Mike Newell's influence; "I come from a school where making it funny is what matters. Mike was obsessed with keeping it real. Every character, no matter how small, has a story, not just three funny lines. It's a romantic film about love and friendship that swims in a sea of jokes."

Curtis chose to omit any mention of the characters' careers, because he didn't think a group of friends would realistically discuss their jobs while together at a wedding.

Cormier R (2016) 15 Splendid Facts About Four Weddings and a Funeral, Mental Floss website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://www.mentalfloss.com/article/73509/15-splendid-facts-about-four-weddings-and-funeral 
Thompson A (1994) ''Four Weddings and a Funeral'' a surprise hit, Entertainment Weekly website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://ew.com/article/1994/05/06/four-weddings-and-funeral-surprise-hit/ 

Richard Whalley Anthony Curtis, CBE (born 8 November 1956) is New Zealand-born British screenwriter, music producer, actor and film director, known primarily for romantic comedy films such as Four Weddings and a Funeral, Bridget Jones's Diary, Notting Hill, and Love Actually, as well as the hit sitcoms Blackadder, Mr. Bean and The Vicar of Dibley. He is also the founder of the British charity Comic Relief.

Richard Curtis is probably the most successful comedy writer in the world due to one of his many characters: Mr. Bean. He created the 15-show series with actor Rowan Atkinson and it has become one of the most successful British cultural exports in history with hundreds of millions of fans worldwide. 
He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
His fascination with love stories began at age seven after he became infatuated with a girl who rejected him. He remained obsessed for two years. “I was always an absolute fool for love... somewhere deep inside, that is a problem," he said, adding. "Definitely, there was some psychological freakery there.' 
At 13, he won a scholarship to private school Harrow, where he excelled, and left as ‘head boy’. He also won a place at Oxford University where he became friends with Mr. Bean actor Rowan Atkinson and they worked together on Atkinson’s Edinburgh Fringe show.  
After graduation, Richard co-wrote the British comedy classic Blackadder, which ran through the '80s with Atkinson as its star. The program also cemented the comedy careers of Stephen Fry and Hugh Laurie. 
In 1989, Richard wrote his first comedy movie: The Tall Guy starring Jeff Goldblum and Emma Thompson. By the ‘90s he was a big name in British comedy and an international celebrity when his low-budget movie Four Weddings and a Funeral became a runaway success. He followed that with the first Mr. Bean movie, then broke box office records again with the Julia Roberts and Grant movie Notting Hill (1999).
 The hits kept on rolling though the 2000s as he has worked with stars such as Alan Rickman, Emma Thompson, Liam Neeson, and Dustin Hoffman.  
 He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
Richard’s charity work started almost by accident. Like many others, he’d been deeply affected by Live Aid. “I happened to go to dinner and met a girl who ran a charity and was about to go out to Ethiopia,” he said. He went with her and found the experience ‘startling’.  
“I was there towards the end of the famine, but I saw things of such horror that I couldn’t stand by and do nothing. You’d have a situation where people were separated into different corrugated iron huts - one would have people who were going to die that night, the next would be people who were probably going to die - and the last hut was people who might just survive.” 
He never got over it. When he returned to London, he decided to do everything he could to make comedy contribute to aid - from stage shows, to TV, to merchandise,  as if he were the CEO of a business. 

Comic Relief’s aim is to create a just world, free from poverty. That’s also the aim of the campaign group Make Poverty History, which Richard also founded. It’s a coalition of aid agencies and campaign groups that lobby to cancel Third World debt among other goals. He’s pointedly aware of the “slightly peculiar contradiction in my life between the Comic Relief side and the comedy writing side,” but has an explanation. 
“What we do on Red Nose Day is make things as funny as we can so that people can see the other side to all the sadness we show,” he said. “I suppose what we are fighting for is that everyone should have the privilege of leading a happy life and being able to laugh like the rest of us instead of being worried that their husband is going to beat them up or they are going to die of a mosquito bite.”
Richard considers Comic Relief and Make Poverty History to be his main priorities even at the expense of his personal life. “I help edit almost every appeal film we do,” he said in 2019.  “When I see a film about a child who hasn’t got a mosquito net, and the impact that makes, I’ll then write to Kiera Knightley and ask her to do something to help us scale the impact.”

Question: What advice would you give to someone starting out?

1. A very brief background of the writer and the films known for writing


Richard Curtis - who was born in New Zealand and moved to London when he was 11 - has been one of the most influential, profitable, and talked about figures in British comedy. 
The talented 67-year-old has written more rom-coms than Sainsbury's stocks popcorn for with classics such as Four Weddings and a Funeral, Notting Hill, and Love Actually all coming from his fair hand.
In addition to the epic filmography, Richard also penned Blackadder, Mr. Bean and The Vicar of Dibley.
When he's not tickling your funny bones, he is the co-founder of British charity Comic Relief along with comedian Lenny Henry.
As if that's not enough for his CV, Richard Curtis has also been awarded a CBE by the Queen, won two BAFTAs and been nominated for an Academy Award... Is there no stopping him?!

2. Identify and define the writer’s approach to the screenwriting process?


Curtis has described himself as the ‘luckiest man in movies’ for having the chance to write multiple redrafts – with the assistance of his partner Emma Freud – and the power to influence casting choices, select the director, and to intervene during the shooting and editing process.
He has also made no denial that his scripts are largely based on his own career and personal experiences.
He only writes ‘on something he was a hundred percent sure of and no one in the world knew more about than him’.


Leggott J (2012) ‘Travels in Curtisland: Richard Curtis and British Comedy Cinema’, British Comedy Cinema, 184-195, Routledge.


3. What narrative structures and themes does the writer typically use within their writing and films?


Curtis-ian elements of cinematic comedy were present right from the start. First, there is the classic “fish out of water” hero…Next, there is the “love interest,” a thoroughly modern young woman…And finally, there is the “last act dash”...Those same essential elements, held together by an apparently unceasing stream of jokes and one-liners... would be used by Curtis in his hugely successful romantic comedies.
That there are enough narrative, thematic and tonal similarities between all of his major films to establish Curtis as an auteur is usually taken as a given...a philosophy about love and human dependency that had simmered throughout the entire Curtis oeuvre.
Romantic comedy is the main genre, but Curtis injects social commentary and explores cultural clashes.
He uses elements like pop culture references, music, and high/low culture clashes to generate humor and drama. (e.g., contrasting references to David Cassidy and John Lennon with the National Film Theatre in "Four Weddings")
There's some variation in tone and style between films ("The Girl in the Cafe" vs. "The Boat That Rocked").
There's a recurring theme of the value and commoditization of art (e.g., "Bean" with Whistler's Mother painting).

Leggott J (2012) ‘Travels in Curtisland: Richard Curtis and British Comedy Cinema’, British Comedy Cinema, 184-195, Routledge.

Curtis's insights into the screenwriting process highlight the importance of authenticity, personal connection to the material, and the power of collaboration. He values storytelling that resonates with audiences on an emotional level while incorporating elements of humor and wit.

6. Identify and Analyse at least one of the writers well-known screenplays and discuss the different strategies and approaches to their writing, including:


Movie: FOUR WEDDINGS AND A FUNERAL (1994)


Logline: Over the course of five social occasions, a committed bachelor must consider the notion that he may have discovered love.


Synopsis: Lovable Englishman Charles (Hugh Grant) and his group of friends seem to be unlucky in love. When Charles meets a beautiful American named Carrie (Andie MacDowell) at a wedding, he thinks his luck may have changed. But, after one magical night, Carrie returns to the States, ending what might have been. As Charles and Carrie's paths continue to cross - over a handful of nuptials and one funeral - he comes to believe they are meant to be together, even if their timing always seems to be off.


1. Structure
Organization: The film is structured around five major events - four weddings and a funeral.
Settings: Primarily takes place in England, with weddings at various locations and a recurring pub where the friends gather.
Characters:
An ensemble cast of friends in their 30s, including the commitment-phobic Charles (Hugh Grant)
The American beauty Carrie (Andie MacDowell)
A supportive group with their own romantic entanglements.
There's a notable inclusion of a healthy gay couple, Gareth (Simon Callow) and Matthew (John Hannah).
Stage Directions: not applicable
Special Features: The film's charm lies in its witty dialogue and relatable characters, not special effects.

2. Story

Given Circumstances: Charles, a charming but commitment-averse man, finds himself repeatedly drawn to Carrie at weddings.
Dramatic Action: The story follows Charles' emotional journey as he navigates missed opportunities, self-doubt, and ultimately, pursuing true love. A central event is the unexpected death of a friend, Gareth, which adds a layer of grief and reflection.

3. Pace & Character Arcs

Pace: The film cleverly balances humor and emotional moments, with a light and breezy pace at the weddings, punctuated by the more serious funeral scene.
Character Status & Function:
Charles: Protagonist, overcoming his fear of commitment.
Carrie: Love interest, initially hesitant about commitment.
Supporting characters: Each friend has their own quirks and romantic struggles, adding depth and humor.
Relationships: The film explores the complexities of friendship, love, and facing adulthood.
Objectives & Obstacles: Charles desires a committed relationship with Carrie, but his own indecisiveness and her past relationships create hurdles.
Character Arcs: Most characters experience growth. Charles learns to commit, Carrie embraces vulnerability, and the friends find solace in each other.

4. Text Structure & Dialogue

Text Structure: Dialogue-driven, with witty banter and emotional exchanges between characters.
Function of Dialogue: Reveals characters' personalities, drives the plot, and provides humor and emotional depth.
Word Choice: Clever, witty, and sprinkled with British slang.
Characteristics of Dialogue: Fast-paced, natural-sounding conversations that feel realistic.

5. Themes & Genre

Themes: Love, commitment, friendship, facing mortality, the importance of human connection.
Genre: Romantic comedy with dramatic elements.
Idea: Finding love and happiness can be messy and unpredictable, but true connection is worth the journey.
Philosophy: Life is a mix of joy, sorrow, and self-discovery.
Atmosphere: Upbeat and humorous, with moments of poignant reflection.
Central Image: The repeated motif of weddings and a funeral symbolizes life's milestones and the importance of cherishing relationships.
Audience: Primarily targets adults who can relate to the complexities of love and life in your 30s.

Additional information:
Screenwriter Richard Curtis's own experiences as a wedding attendee inspired Four Weddings and a Funeral. According to Curtis he began writing the script at age 34, after realising he had attended 65 weddings in an 11-year period. At one wedding he was propositioned by a fellow guest, but he turned her down and forever regretted it; accordingly he based the origin of Charles and Carrie's romance on that situation.

It took Curtis 19 drafts to reach the final version. He has commented on director Mike Newell's influence; "I come from a school where making it funny is what matters. Mike was obsessed with keeping it real. Every character, no matter how small, has a story, not just three funny lines. It's a romantic film about love and friendship that swims in a sea of jokes."

Curtis chose to omit any mention of the characters' careers, because he didn't think a group of friends would realistically discuss their jobs while together at a wedding.

Thompson A (1994) ''Four Weddings and a Funeral'' a surprise hit, Entertainment Weekly website, accessed 21 March 2024. https://ew.com/article/1994/05/06/four-weddings-and-funeral-surprise-hit/ 

Richard Whalley Anthony Curtis, CBE (born 8 November 1956) is New Zealand-born British screenwriter, music producer, actor and film director, known primarily for romantic comedy films such as Four Weddings and a Funeral, Bridget Jones's Diary, Notting Hill, and Love Actually, as well as the hit sitcoms Blackadder, Mr. Bean and The Vicar of Dibley. He is also the founder of the British charity Comic Relief.

Richard Curtis is probably the most successful comedy writer in the world due to one of his many characters: Mr. Bean. He created the 15-show series with actor Rowan Atkinson and it has become one of the most successful British cultural exports in history with hundreds of millions of fans worldwide. 
He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
His fascination with love stories began at age seven after he became infatuated with a girl who rejected him. He remained obsessed for two years. “I was always an absolute fool for love... somewhere deep inside, that is a problem," he said, adding. "Definitely, there was some psychological freakery there.' 
At 13, he won a scholarship to private school Harrow, where he excelled, and left as ‘head boy’. He also won a place at Oxford University where he became friends with Mr. Bean actor Rowan Atkinson and they worked together on Atkinson’s Edinburgh Fringe show.  
After graduation, Richard co-wrote the British comedy classic Blackadder, which ran through the '80s with Atkinson as its star. The program also cemented the comedy careers of Stephen Fry and Hugh Laurie. 
In 1989, Richard wrote his first comedy movie: The Tall Guy starring Jeff Goldblum and Emma Thompson. By the ‘90s he was a big name in British comedy and an international celebrity when his low-budget movie Four Weddings and a Funeral became a runaway success. He followed that with the first Mr. Bean movie, then broke box office records again with the Julia Roberts and Grant movie Notting Hill (1999).
 The hits kept on rolling though the 2000s as he has worked with stars such as Alan Rickman, Emma Thompson, Liam Neeson, and Dustin Hoffman.  
 He co-founded Comic Relief in 1985, a British charity that has raised more than $1bn by getting famous people to do silly things and by encouraging others to wear clown red noses. 
Richard’s charity work started almost by accident. Like many others, he’d been deeply affected by Live Aid. “I happened to go to dinner and met a girl who ran a charity and was about to go out to Ethiopia,” he said. He went with her and found the experience ‘startling’.  
“I was there towards the end of the famine, but I saw things of such horror that I couldn’t stand by and do nothing. You’d have a situation where people were separated into different corrugated iron huts - one would have people who were going to die that night, the next would be people who were probably going to die - and the last hut was people who might just survive.” 
He never got over it. When he returned to London, he decided to do everything he could to make comedy contribute to aid - from stage shows, to TV, to merchandise,  as if he were the CEO of a business. 

Comic Relief’s aim is to create a just world, free from poverty. That’s also the aim of the campaign group Make Poverty History, which Richard also founded. It’s a coalition of aid agencies and campaign groups that lobby to cancel Third World debt among other goals. He’s pointedly aware of the “slightly peculiar contradiction in my life between the Comic Relief side and the comedy writing side,” but has an explanation. 
“What we do on Red Nose Day is make things as funny as we can so that people can see the other side to all the sadness we show,” he said. “I suppose what we are fighting for is that everyone should have the privilege of leading a happy life and being able to laugh like the rest of us instead of being worried that their husband is going to beat them up or they are going to die of a mosquito bite.”
Richard considers Comic Relief and Make Poverty History to be his main priorities even at the expense of his personal life. “I help edit almost every appeal film we do,” he said in 2019.  “When I see a film about a child who hasn’t got a mosquito net, and the impact that makes, I’ll then write to Kiera Knightley and ask her to do something to help us scale the impact.”
